title:
1.3: States the tree-form output produced by pkt.show() for an Ethernet/IP/TCP packet to show which fields are unset before serialization (e.g., `###[ IP ]###
 ihl = None
 len = None
 chksum = None`).
1.7: States any neighbor/ARP warnings printed during send when no MAC is found to show link-layer resolution behavior (e.g., WARNING: Mac address to reach destination not found. Using broadcast).
1.10: Reports the stacked packet representation showing nested layers (e.g., <Ether type=IPv4 |<IP ... |<TCP ... |>>>) to show how the final structure looks before sending.
1.11: Shows the packet count confirmation as "Sent 1 packets" that Scapy prints after sending to show what Scapy reports during transmission.
1.12: Shows the parsed packet showing that IP len now has a concrete value (e.g., len = 40) to show how Scapy extracts the total length from received bytes.
1.13: Reports the parsed packet summary showing the full layer hierarchy (e.g., Ether / IP / TCP 5.6.7.8:12345 > 1.2.3.4:http S) to demonstrate how Scapy reconstructs layers when showing the packet.
1.14: Identifies the parsed packet showing that IP chksum now has a concrete value (e.g., chksum = 0x6abc) to show how Scapy extracts the checksum from received bytes.
1.15: Reports a summary section that consolidates what Scapy revealed during the three phases (building, sending, sniffing) to show the complete packet lifecycle behavior.
1.16: States that Scapy printed no explicit routing table information during transmission beyond neighbour resolution warnings
2.1: States that IP.chksum or TCP.chksum contain numeric values before calling raw() or sendp()
2.2: States that IP.len or TCP.dataofs show values like 40 or 5 immediately after layer construction
